title: How People Found Their Way Before Satellites Did the Work

lesson.learn:
Before GPS, navigation was a blend of instruments, maps, astronomy, and disciplined observation. Sailors used compasses to keep direction, sextants to measure the altitude of the sun or stars, and charts to estimate position relative to coastlines and currents. On land, travelers relied on landmarks, stars, local knowledge, and carefully copied maps.

The genius of older navigation was not that it was simple. It was that it turned the sky and the world into a coordinate system people could actually use.
lesson.simpler: Before GPS, people used compasses, stars, maps, and landmarks to figure out where they were going. Navigation was learned skill, not magic.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Navigation before GPS worked because people built reliable methods from the sky, the sea, and carefully measured maps.
lesson.deeperDive:
The sextant was revolutionary because it allowed navigators to calculate latitude with real precision. Map projections such as the Mercator projection were equally important because no flat map can perfectly represent a round planet. Every navigation system is therefore a compromise between accuracy, convenience, and the kind of journey being made.

What changed over time was not just the tools, but the confidence they gave travelers. Better instruments reduced uncertainty, and better charts turned dangerous routes into repeatable ones.
lesson.funFact: A sextant can still be useful if the satellites disappear.

people: Navigators - experts in reading position and direction
Cartographers - makers of maps and charts
Astronomers - scientists who turned the sky into a reference system
Sailors - users of practical navigation tools
places: Ocean routes - where celestial navigation mattered most
Harbors - places where charts were copied and corrected
Observatories - sites that improved celestial measurements
Map rooms - where paper navigation was planned
years: Ancient times - stars and landmarks guide travel
17th to 19th centuries - navigation instruments become more precise
20th century - air and sea navigation improve rapidly
21st century - GPS becomes the default system
concepts: Sextant - a tool for measuring angles to celestial objects
Map projection - a way to flatten the globe onto paper
Latitude - distance north or south of the equator
